title: Chapter Ten: The Promise She Can't Keep
content: The chapel smelled like cold stone and burnt wax. I'd walked these corridors a hundred times, knew every twist of worn carpet and flickering sconce between the dormitory wing and the east alcove, but tonight the walk had been longer — each step a mile, each shadow a mouth whispering what I'd done.
He was already there.

I saw him before he saw me. Heath sat in our pew — the front one on the pact-side, where we'd carved our initials into the underside of the kneeler second year, where we'd bled onto the floorboards the night we swore we'd burn Robert Vance to ash. His hands held a hymnal, open to some page he wasn't reading. His thumb traced the edge of the paper, over and over, the way he touched things when his mind was somewhere else — somewhere worse.

The candle at the altar guttered. Wax had pooled on the brass plate, a white scar hardening at the edges. The stained-glass saints threw fractured color across the stone floor, red and blue and gold, but none of it reached him. He sat in the dark between the light.
He looked up.

His face was a wall. Not angry — I could have handled angry. Not sad — I could have carved that out of him. Just closed. Sealed. Like a door I'd never been invited through, except I'd been through it a thousand times before, and the lock had turned while I wasn't watching.
He said nothing.

The silence was so complete I could hear my own pulse, the thin whine of wind through the chapel's cracked window, the tiny hiss of the candle. My throat was dry. I tasted copper — I'd bitten the inside of my cheek on the walk over and hadn't noticed.
"I need to tell you something."

He closed the hymnal. Set it on the pew beside him with a care that felt like a verdict. Then he looked at me again, and something moved behind that wall — one crack, one tremor — before he locked it down.
"Word travels fast at Blackwood, Cat." His voice was low and level, sand over stone. "I already know."

Of course he did. Gossip was this school's second religion, and the chapel was its first. By now, half the pact-side had heard that Catherine Vance had accepted Julian Linton's ring. By morning, the other half would know too.
But hearing it and understanding it were different things. I needed him to understand.

I slid into the pew beside him. Not close — a foot of cold wood between us, the hymnal a barricade I didn't dare move. The wood was hard under my thighs, unforgiving. My hands folded in my lap like I was about to pray, which was a joke, because the only person I'd ever prayed to was sitting right next to me and he wasn't listening.
"It's not what you think."
"You're engaged to Julian Linton." He said the name like it tasted wrong. "What am I supposed to think?"
I took a breath. The air was thin and old in here, centuries of incense and regret soaked into the stone. "I'm going to use him."

Heath's jaw tightened. A micro-movement. I caught it because I'd memorized the architecture of his face years ago — every twitch, every clench, every way he betrayed himself when he was trying not to.

"I'm going to get inside the Linton family's financial structure," I said. "Their holdings, their debts, their connections to Robert and the board. Julian's the door. I walk through it, I find the evidence we need, and I burn the whole thing down." I heard my own voice — steady, mechanical, like I was reciting a formula. "I'll come back, Heath. I'm not pretending anything. I'm going to war."

For a long moment, he just looked at me. The candlelight caught the edge of his cheekbone, the hollow beneath his eye. He smelled like cedar and smoke — he'd been outside earlier, I realized. Standing in the cold. Thinking about this before I ever walked through the door.
"You're going to war," he repeated. Not a question. Not agreement either. Something between wonder and grief — the way you'd look at someone who told you they could walk through fire and not burn.
"Yes."
"Inside his house."
"Inside his— yes."

"With his ring on your finger. Wearing his name. Sleeping under his roof." Each word landed separately, like stones dropped into a well, and I heard each one hit bottom. "You think you can pretend to love someone and not lose yourself in the lie?"
"I'm not going to pretend to love him."
"No? What are you going to do — file reports from the marital bed?"
The image hit me like a slap — heat flooding my face, my chest, somewhere lower and more ashamed. I pushed it down. "I'll do what I have to."
"You think you can live in his house, wear his ring, let him hold you at night" — his voice cracked on *hold*, the wall shuddering — "and still come back to me?"
"Yes."
The word came out too fast. Too sure. The kind of certainty that announces its own doubt. We both heard it.

Heath leaned back against the pew. The wood creaked. His hands lay open on his thighs, palms up, empty — the posture he took when he was forcing himself not to clench them into fists. "You're not a weapon, Cat."
The words landed wrong. Not where he aimed them — somewhere else entirely, some chamber in my chest I'd bricked over years ago.
"You're a person," he said. "People break."

I heard *You're not a weapon* and my stupid, wounded brain translated it instantly: *You're not strong enough.* I heard *People break* and it became: *I think you'll break.* I heard doubt — the first doubt he'd ever shown in me — and it rewrote itself as abandonment. As Heath Cruz putting distance between us before I could drag him down.
"I won't break," I said. My voice was harder now. Sharper. A blade honed on the whetstone of my own fear.
"That's what everyone says before they do."
"I'm not everyone."

"No." He looked at me then — really looked, not the wall, not the shuttered distance, but the raw thing underneath, and his eyes were dark and furious and terrified. "You're not. You're Catherine Vance, and you think being the one who hurts means you can't be the one who's hurt. You think if you call it strategy, it won't cost you. But it costs you every time, Cat. It costs you and you don't even see the bill."

My hands were shaking. I pressed them flat against my thighs, let the rough wool bite into my palms. The scar tissue on my left hand ached — the old burn from the night of the pact, the skin never quite right since. Phantom pain. Memory made physical.
"What if you start being Catherine Linton," he said, "and forget how to be Catherine Vance?"
"I won't."
"You don't know that."

Three words. Quiet. Final. And the worst part — the part that split me open like a rib spread — was that he was right. I didn't know. I couldn't know. You can't stand in two houses at once. You can't wear two names and not feel the seam between them, the place where one ends and the other begins, blurring like ink in rain. He was right, and the truth of it sat in my throat like a stone I couldn't swallow or spit out.

Because if I didn't come back — if the months stretched and the mask became the face — then I'd chosen Julian over him. That was the debt he was planting in the space between us, silent and growing. And if I did come back, if I crawled home with the evidence and the victory and the war won — I'd still have been someone else's. I'd still have lived in another man's house, worn another man's name, let another man touch what Heath considered his. Not possessive. Not ownership. Something worse — something earned, something built through years of shared danger and shared silence and the quiet gravity of two people who chose each other when no one else would.
The cost didn't cancel out. It compounded.

I opened my mouth to argue, to find the words that would make him see — this is strategy, this is sacrifice, this is the move that wins the game — but nothing came. Just the stale chapel air and the tick of cooling wax and the sound of my own ragged breath.

Heath watched me struggle. He saw the argument die in my throat, and something in his expression shifted — not softer, not kinder. Emptier. Like he'd already started letting go, practicing the motion the way you practice a keystroke until your fingers remember and your mind can stop.
He nodded.

That was worse than anything he could have said. A nod — agreement to something I hadn't proposed, acceptance of a version of events where I walked away and he stayed here in the dark, alone. He didn't believe me. He didn't believe I'd come back whole or come back at all. And he was nodding anyway, because what else could he do? Chain me to the chapel? Become the thing that trapped me, the cage I'd need to escape? He'd rather lose me than be the reason I stayed broken.
"If you go," he said, "you go alone."
The words hit the air like a verdict read aloud.

"I won't be your insurance policy. I won't wait like a dog at the door — checking every footstep in the corridor, wondering if tonight's the night you come back different, or if tonight's the night you don't come back at all." His voice was sand and splintered wood. "I can't do that, Cat. I won't."
"I'm not asking you to wait."

The lie came out smooth. Practiced. I'd rehearsed it on the walk over — *I'm not asking you to wait* — and the delivery was flawless, which made it worse, because we both heard how clean it was, how prepared, and real things aren't clean. Real things snag and bleed.
"Good." He stared at the altar. The candle reflected in his eyes, a small trembling flame. "Because I won't."

Another lie. Bigger. The biggest either of us had ever told, and we'd told some monstrous lies in our time — to teachers, to enemies, to ourselves. But this one was the size of a cathedral, and it echoed in the silence between us like a stone dropped in an empty room.
He wouldn't wait. He wouldn't check the corridor. He wouldn't lie awake counting the days. He wouldn't. And I wasn't asking him to. And we were both so full of shit we could hardly breathe.
The silence that followed wasn't peace. It was the silence after a structure collapses — the dust still falling, the settling, the awful pause before the second wave comes down.

We sat like that for a long time. I don't know how long. The chapel had no clock, and I refused to check my phone, because checking would mean admitting that time was passing, that this moment had an end, that eventually one of us would have to stand up and walk away and make the leaving real. The pew was hard beneath me. My spine ached from holding myself upright, from not leaning into him, from not letting my shoulder brush his even though there was barely a foot of space between us. The air was cold and close, thick with the ghost of incense. The candle at the altar guttered and steadied, guttered and steadied, a heartbeat I could have timed my own against.
He didn't touch me. I didn't touch him. We sat in the dark like two planets in the same orbit — close enough to feel the pull, too proud and too shattered to crash.

My scar throbbed. The burn on my palm, the one we'd given each other that night — his hand over mine, the flame, the sizzle of skin, the pact sealed in tissue damage and blood. I pressed my hand flat against the wood of the pew and felt nothing but cold grain. The scar remembered what the rest of me was trying to forget.

At some point — minutes, hours, I couldn't tell — the candle guttered again and didn't steady. The flame shrank to a blue pinprick, swayed, and went out. Smoke curled upward, thin and grey, and the chapel fell into full dark. Only the stained-glass saints now, illuminated by whatever floodlight the school kept trained on the east wall, casting their fractured colors across the floor like a promise no one intended to keep.
I stood.

My legs were stiff, half-asleep, and the first step I took was unsteady. My hand found the back of the pew for balance. I didn't look at Heath. I couldn't. If I looked at him, I'd see the wall again — or worse, I'd see behind it, the ruin he was hiding, and then I'd stay. I'd sit back down. I'd tell him I was sorry, I was wrong, I'd find another way. And there was no other way. I'd turned every stone, checked every angle, and Julian Linton was the only door that opened into the room where Robert Vance kept his secrets.

So I walked. Down the aisle, past the pews where generations of Blackwood students had pretended to pray, past the font of holy water that hadn't been blessed in years, past the kneeler with our initials carved underneath. The stone floor was freezing through the thin soles of my shoes. Each step echoed — I heard it, and I knew he heard it too, every footfall a countdown.

I reached the door. Heavy oak, iron hinges, a threshold worn smooth by centuries of frightened children and hollow prayers. I put my hand on the iron latch and felt the cold bite into my skin, sharp and clean, the kind of cold that reminds you you're still alive.
I didn't look back.
But I stopped.

At the threshold — the liminal space, the between — I stopped, and I pressed my scarred palm flat against the doorframe. Not for him to see. He couldn't see, not from where he was sitting in the dark. But something deeper than sight, something below language, below reason — a frequency only we could hear. *I'm still me. I'm still the girl who bled with you in this room. Remember that, even when I can't.*
The wood was rough against the scar tissue. I held it there for one second. Two. Three. Then I let go.
I walked out into the dark corridor. The door swung shut behind me, a soft click of the latch, final as a period at the end of a sentence.
The corridor was cold and empty and long, and I walked it alone.

Behind me, in the chapel, I imagined him still sitting in the pew. The hymnal beside him. The burned-out candle. The silence. And somewhere under the kneeler, our initials carved into wood — CV and HC, the letters of a pact that predicated everything on staying, on holding the line, on never becoming the thing we swore to destroy.

The pact was cracking. I could feel it — a fault line running through the foundation of everything I'd built my certainty on. And I was walking into a cage I'd built myself, gold bars and velvet lining, and he was watching me go, and neither of us could stop it.

The blizzard was coming. I could taste it in the air — that ozone sharpness before a storm, the pressure drop that makes your ears pop and your old wounds whine. It was coming, and I was walking toward it, and the only person who'd ever stood between me and the cold was sitting alone in a dark chapel with a hymnal he wasn't reading and a promise he knew I couldn't keep.
I walked faster.
The corridor swallowed me whole.
